speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five in the interview with Maria Glickman. Where did you go from Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Riga, we did go to Poland. We did go with-- with a train through Vilna, Minsk, Brest-Litovsk. This was the end of the-- the-- Russia. From there, we did go to Biala Podlaska. This was the first place in Poland. We stood there three days and nights. The cardinal-- you know what a cardinal-- he came to see my children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He put his hand over them, and he said, you came with God, and you will go with God, and you will be happy. And he gave them packages with chocolate like this, both of them. This was-- I felt very happy of it. It means that not just the government, but the Holy Father from Poland was good to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life was better in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, yeah, much better. In Poland, you got right away work. You made money. And I played a little bit Jewish theater concerts. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Polish people interested in your story? Did you ever tell--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they know everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told them your stories, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have-- they did know all themselves what did go on. They have seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't ask you about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, some asked, some not. They've seen-- Russia was terrible, what they did. It's-- it's not-- Russia had 600,000 people. 90% of them got killed. They had to see. But they helped us. They gave us money to buy furniture. They gave us-- my girlfriend left Poland for Israel. They gave us the apartments where there's a new house. Otherwise, you have to stay two, three years and wait. They gave us estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: All through the government?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the government. Right away, I came, I signed the papers, they gave me the keys, and it's mine. Yeah. And my husband worked there. We were there from-- in Poland from March 12 to August 27. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was March-- March 12, '57. We left '58, August 27.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel. I had my mother and my sister there. So it took a little bit of time to go by ship, by train and by ship. We came. I had-- my cousin was a-- a big person in Israel. He was an ambassador in Brazil, in Argentina, in Paris. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said to me, your cousin is [NON-ENGLISH]. You will have a good life. But it wasn't like that. But-- it was hot, and I was sick with my heart. Because of my feet, I got sick with my heart, my valve. And I couldn't stand the heat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked two and a half years in a prehistoric museum for the government. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took me in because-- not because they did know me, but my girlfriend worked by the mayor of the city. And they did know that I speak a couple of languages. And people would come from all over the world, so I could talk to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I worked two and a half years. I made very little money. I had to make $600. They paid me $250. Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I worked two works. I would come early in the morning, [INAUDIBLE], clean the museum-- two museums. Biologic museum upstairs, and on the second floor, the prehistoric museum. And then I would sit and write down who came, who lived, who-- from where it is, what they looked for, what they want to know. I worked two and a half years there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we-- eight months before we left, my cousin said, if-- I don't tell you to come from Israel here. But if you feel that you must, I will send you the papers. And in that time, you had to have, they call it quota. You had to have a quota. My husband had. I didn't, because the Latvian people, they lived a very rich life in Europe. They didn't want to go to no place. So they had free places. So we came here. After four and a half months, came here. I have a wonderful girlfriend. She-- I had already an apartment, furniture, food, and work for my husband. We came December 27. And December-- January 2, my husband worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came December 27, in '65. January the 2nd in '66, he worked already. And since then, he worked. But he got sick at work in '72. He got a stroke. He was 57 years old. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then the whole luck changed. We had a house. We had to sell it because I didn't have money to hold the house. Now they said they will give us pension. We don't get it. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who said they would give you pension?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did go to a lawyer, and from the lawyers, the Claims Conference. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got three letters from Germany that I got-- in '72 we get to $7,340, me, and my husband, $7,350 in '71. So what we did with the money is we bought the house. And the rest, my daughter got sick-- very sick, in Israel. She got tuberculosis. And I said to my husband, we have $5,000. I'm going. Two small children. So I came there. And thank to God she is alive and everything is all right. But I was three months with the children, and I need the money. No more money, but they don't give us money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children-- my daughter is director in Workmen's Circle in Elizabeth. My younger daughter works for CBS. She's a coordinator and producer. They're very intelligent girls, both. They learned. They know what they want. This is all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your experiences during the war had any effect on the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. The young one, 100%, the younger one.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In every way. She lives with it. She likes the people from there. She likes to hear everything. She's-- she's in the organization for the people who had children after the war from the concentration camp. She-- she speaks perfectly Yiddish, like me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She is-- she got more than my older daughter, because my daughter-- my older daughter came in '75 here. Because when we left, she was married, two children. He wanted to go. She didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you think her involvement is a result of the way you raised her, or what you told her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your children what had happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I did. Of course I did. They had to know. Sometimes, could be I was not together. I mean, I would say something. But later on, I would remember what I said, and I tell them why I said it. That where I walk, where I sit, when I sleep, everything did go with me. I live with it. It's-- it's a terrible thing, but I would walk out in the middle of the night and walk in the dark street, not to lie alone in bed. I can't help it. Now as then, there is nothing to wait for. Not young any more.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I just think sometimes, why didn't I feel that other girls-- young girls felt, to have a young man, to be loved? You know, everybody liked me when I was a child. But later on, there-- everybody looked for himself how to get a piece of food, something. This was my life. What do you think sustained you? What helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to survive. I did everything. I was-- it was-- one time I slipped by a Polish lady five days, me and my girlfriend and her brother and his mother. We run, you know? We didn't know what to do. So we run here, we run there. We tried to ask people to help us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, people didn't want help me. They don't-- they didn't believe me that I'm Jewish. I came to ask for buying-- buy bread. The lady said, I don't have bread. I said, why not? Two ladies came just out-- Jewish ladies-- they bought bread. She said, it's not true. So I open and I show her the-- the yellow thing. She said, are you Jewish? I says, of course I'm Jewish. Oh, she says, I will give you bread. You understand? Jews-- this goes with you. I have forgotten many things. Could be I have forgotten half of it. But a little bit was inside, and it stays with me. Can't help it. Do you ever dream about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not anymore. Since I had my open heart surgery, no. Nothing at all. Before this, yes, every night. I fall. I run. They shot me. Killed. Not anymore. No. Since '81, I don't dream about nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have great grandchildren now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message-- is there something you would like to say to them and to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like that they should be who they were born. They shouldn't change their religion. They should think about what can happen if you forget who you are. I don't-- I like people, all kinds of people. But everybody has to remember that he has his roots. And I want them to have their roots. Their mother is born in Israel, and they are born here. They're very smart children, and beautiful, both of them. Really. That's all. I love very much my granddaughter. She's very smart, intelligent, and good. And I love very much my grandson. He's finishing lawyer next year. My family knows the main thing is to learn. If you don't learn, you have nothing. If you don't have much money but you have, here, something that you can give to other people, it's very-- it's very important. I think so. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is it. My younger daughter, she says, Mother, I love you. You know I love you. I says, thank you very much. But when I talk to you, I laugh. And everybody laughs there. I say, it's good. To laugh is healthy when we speak in Yiddish. So I-- I say something, and she laughs. So the people who [INAUDIBLE] they love you, so It's better than to cry. I cried enough. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I am happy that I didn't cry here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to add to your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this moment, I don't remember. Could be there are many things, but [INAUDIBLE]. Don't remember. The only thing that was terrible when I found my mother. When my mother came to pick me up in Israel, [SIGHS] she didn't recognize me. I didn't recognize her. But my daughter said, Mother, your grandmother is asking for you by radio. I said, yes. I don't-- didn't hear. She says, here. So I heard. And I looked around. And my mother's standing behind me. She says, shhh. I shouldn't cry and not talk. And she lost her voice. She didn't talk all day long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nerves. And my sister came at night, because her husband was working, she was working. She came at night, and my daughter was in the balcony by my mother. And she said, Mother, Aunt Asya is coming. She recognized her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I rang down, and my mother was living in a city where the real Hasidim live, in Bnei Brak. And everybody pulled out their head to see what-- what will go on. And my sister falled to my feet and kissed my feet. And I couldn't take it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This was a terrible thing. Do you want to mention something about your sister now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My sister is a wonderful person, first of all, for everybody. If somebody needs something, she would be the first one to help. She's very intelligent. She is a poet. What they say in Israel, they compare it to Goethe. She's a wonderful poet. Six and a half years ago, she got sick, and she did go to have operation, and she never walked out. She got paralyzed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was then-- six and a half years ago-- 56 1/2 years old. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] She has a wonderful husband. That's true. Of course, now she has three nurses a day, for 24 hours. It's very good to hear. This is the only luck that she has, because she had lost-- she had three sons. All three died, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one in New York, one in Philadelphia, and one in Israel. Because he was sent from the hospital to teach. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I don't care. Let him knock. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] five abortions. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not-- she didn't, by herself. So she's alone. She's always alone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now what I know, my daughter talked with my brother on a Sunday, that she stopped to speak. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you would like to add at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. About?

speaker: interviewer
text: About your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: My life. I had a very hard life. Believe me. I was steps and-- and-- and floors and toilets in Israel. And everything that I could, just to have a little bit money for food. Because my husband didn't make enough money. I had it very hard. And this made me to leave Israel, too. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My daughter, my older daughter, wasn't with us. She was in Kibbutz. And the younger was with me. They didn't take the younger, because she was too young. They took just from 12 years, and she was 10. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was my life. The only thing, I had friends, many friends. From the ghetto, from concentration camp, people where I lived. I had always friends. I wasn't alone. And now I am in the United States, and I'm always alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My husband is in the hospital, two years. January 15, it will be two years. So I'm always alone. My daughters work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: For all my luck, I came here. What can I do? Some people don't have anywhere to sleep. So I have to say thank you. And I had what to eat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was very sick in this country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just came a year. In a month and a half, I got sick. I had a mastectomy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And later on, we got to know that I didn't have cancer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

words.text: And I had what to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 Years later, I had an operation on my breast. My girlfriend was working there-- doctor. She said, no cancer. No taking of the breast. And then-- this was in '76. And in '81, I had open heart surgery. And now, October 29, I had one oper-- two operations. In 30 I had a [INAUDIBLE] operation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyway, I hold-- I'm holding my own. [LAUGHS] I want to live up to 80, at least. We'll see. I think this is enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are very grateful, and thank you very much for sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Jewish man in the Vilna ghetto had to wear the-- how is it in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Star of David.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Star of David. And I wear them, too. I sew it myself. It's not something that they make for us. You had to do it yourself. When I came to the concentration camp, they took away my clothing, there it was. So because of this, I had it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother, taken in '45, in Siberia. Her name was [? Marjette ?] Kuricki Sonia [? Basel. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my friend, Musia Dajches. She was an actress. This picture was taken in '32, sent to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture, taken by me in '93 in Vilna, where it was the ghetto. Now it's just this piece of the ghetto. It was taken on the [INAUDIBLE] 16. The street is [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is on the wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the wall is written, where it was-- was it was in this place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of me and both my daughters. The bigger one is [? Mosia, ?] and the smaller one is Sima. [? Mosia ?] is born in '45, and Sima is born in '48. Taking the picture is in Kaiserwald, where I was in concentration camp. That's all. This is a picture of my husband, Hanon Glickman. Make the picture in Haifa in '65, in April month. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in '56, before my friend left Vilna. It's in Ponary, in the grave where the Jewish people were killed. There is my friend, [? Hasia, ?] her mother-in-law, [INAUDIBLE], her friend, Misha, and her friend, Rosa, and her daughter, Miriam. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in '93. It's in the same place where the picture come from '56, in Ponary, in the grave. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in '40-- in '45. It's a monument in Ponary. But it was re-erected-- re-erected, and it was taken off the Hebrew Jewish words, and now is just in Lithuanian and Russian, written that here are people killed by the Germans. That here are people killed by the Germans, that they were Jews. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is in Jerusalem, by my sister in her house. It's my sister, Asya Gay, and her husband, Dr. Isaac Gay. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my sister's book, written in poems. And the book's name is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the book written?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the '70s. The date, I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in the United States, in Irvington, by a wedding of a friend's son, me and my husband. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my family, taken when my grandson had bar mitzvah. There is my daughter, Sima, from left, my granddaughter, Rifka, my daughter, Molly, my husband, Hanon, my grandson, Leor, me, my son-in-law, Nathan, and his cousin. That's all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all. This is a picture made here in Springfield. It's my daughter, her grandson-- do I say the name? Adam. And her husband, Nathan, and her granddaughter-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't have the name. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My great grander-- Lemor. Granddaughter, Lemor. Nervous.
